1: The Spirit of God (rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm) comes upon Azariah son of Oded. The prophetic Spirit clothing a human agent is the standard formula for OT prophetic inspiration (1 Chronicles 12:18; 2 Chronicles 20:14; Judges 6:34). Azariah son of Oded is otherwise unknown — the Chronicler often introduces minor prophetic figures who speak crucial theological words and then disappear from the record. This anonymity underscores that the word’s authority comes from its divine source, not the prophet’s status.
2: Šimʿûnî ʾAsāʾ wᵉḵol-Yᵉhûḏāh wBinyāmin YHWH ʿimmᵉḵem bihyôtḵem ʿimmô wᵉʾim-tiḏrᵉšûhû yimmāṣēʾ lāḵem wᵉʾim-taʿăzᵉḇûhû yaʿăzōḇ ʾetḵem — “Hear me, Asa, and all Judah and Benjamin: The LORD is with you while you are with him. If you seek him (tiḏrᵉšûhû), he will be found by you, but if you forsake him (taʿăzᵉḇûhû), he will forsake you.” The divine mutuality formula — presence conditioned on covenant faithfulness — is the Chronicler’s most economical statement of his entire theology. The conditional is not a threat but a covenant structure: YHWH’s presence is with those who seek him; his absence accompanies those who forsake him.
3: For a long time Israel was without the true God (ʾĕlōhê ʾemet), without a teaching priest (kōhēn môreh), and without law (tôrāh). The triple absence — God, priestly teacher, Torah — describes the period of the judges (the likely referent) or the condition of the northern kingdom. The three together form the covenant community’s minimal infrastructure: divine presence, authoritative teaching, written covenant. Without any one, the community is spiritually displaced.
4: But when in their distress they turned to YHWH, the God of Israel, and sought him (wayyᵉḇaqšūhû), he was found by them. The historical precedent of Judges — the cyclical pattern of apostasy, distress, return, restoration — is cited as proof that the seeking God of Azariah’s oracle has a track record. Past seeking-and-finding underwrites the present promise: Azariah is not offering a new covenant principle but reminding Asa of the already-established divine pattern.
5: In those times there was no peace (šālôm) for the one who went out or the one who came in, for great disturbances (mᵉhûmōt rabbôt) afflicted all the inhabitants of the lands. The description of social chaos during the period of unfaithfulness — instability for traveler and resident alike — draws on the historical memory of the judges era and the political fragmentation of the early monarchy. Peace (šālôm) and rest are gifts of covenant faithfulness; their absence marks covenant disruption.
6: They were broken in pieces, nation against nation and city against city, for God troubled them with every sort of distress. The universal social disintegration — gôy bᵉgôy ûʿîr bᵉʿîr (nation against nation, city against city) — describes a condition where the bonds of community have dissolved. The attribution to divine causation (ʾĕlōhîm hᵉhāmām bᵉḵol-ṣārāh) is the prophetic historical perspective: social chaos is not merely political failure but covenant consequence.
7: Wᵉʾattem ḥizqû wᵉʾal-yirpû yᵉdêḵem kî yēš śāḵār lipᵉʿullāṯᵉḵem — “But you, take courage! Do not let your hands be weak, for your work will be rewarded (śāḵār lipᵉʿullāṯᵉḵem).” The closing charge to Asa — positive, forward-looking — balances the historical warning of vv3-6. The three elements: ḥizqû (be strong), ʾal-yirpû (do not be slack), and the promise of reward (śāḵār) for covenant labor. The covenant is not only about avoiding judgment but about receiving the positive fruits of faithfulness.
8: When Asa hears the words of Azariah the prophet, he takes courage and removes the abominable idols from all the land of Judah and Benjamin and from the cities that he had captured in the hill country of Ephraim — including restoring the altar of YHWH in front of the vestibule. The reform expands beyond Judah’s original territory to include the cities captured from the north (2 Chronicles 13) — an all-Israel scope that reflects the Chronicler’s pan-Israelite vision. The restored altar is the cultic heart of the reform.
9: Asa gathers all Judah and Benjamin, and those from Ephraim, Manasseh, and Simeon who sojourn with them — for great numbers had deserted (nāpᵉlû) from Israel to him when they saw that YHWH his God was with him. Northerners streaming south to participate in the Judahite reform is the Chronicler’s all-Israel motif at its clearest: covenantal faithfulness in the south creates a magnetic draw for those in the north who want to serve YHWH properly. The Chronicler consistently shows the good Judahite king attracting those from the northern tribes who retain covenant loyalty.
10: They assemble at Jerusalem in the third month of the fifteenth year of Asa’s reign. The third month is significant: it corresponds to the time of Shavuot (Pentecost), the feast celebrating the giving of the Torah at Sinai (Exodus 19:1). The covenant renewal assembly in the month of Torah-giving links Asa’s reform to the original covenant institution — as if the nation is returning to Sinai through its re-commitment in Jerusalem.
11: They sacrifice to YHWH that day 700 bulls and 7,000 sheep from the spoil they have brought. The sacrificial quantities come directly from the Cushite campaign spoil (2 Chronicles 14:13-14) — the material blessing of YHWH’s victory is immediately consecrated back to YHWH in sacrifice. The cycle is complete: YHWH gives rest and victory; the people give back the fruits of victory as sacrificial worship. Gratitude expressed through sacrifice is the Chronicler’s ideal of covenant reciprocity.
12: They enter into a covenant (wayyāḇōʾû ḇabᵉrît) to seek YHWH (liḏrôš ʾet-YHWH) with all their heart (bᵉḵol-lēḇāḇ) and with all their soul (ûḇᵉḵol-napšām). The covenantal seeking formula combines the two anthropological terms — heart and soul (= Deuteronomy 6:5’s lēḇāḇ and nepeš) — in the complete self-dedication formula. The covenant renewal is not a legal compliance exercise but a total-self commitment to YHWH.
13: Whoever would not seek YHWH, the God of Israel, should be put to death, whether young or old, man or woman. The death penalty for covenant apostasy (Deuteronomy 13:6-18; 2 Chronicles 17:12) is the covenant’s self-protective provision: the community cannot survive if those who reject YHWH remain within it. The covenant is not a private option but a communal structure; its integrity depends on communal enforcement. The Chronicler records this as Asa’s act without editorial comment — covenant renewal involves hard edges.
14: They swear to YHWH with a loud voice and with shouting and with trumpets and with horns. The multi-sensory, full-community oath — voices, shouting, trumpets, horns — is the public, irreversible declaration of covenant commitment. The loud voice (bᵉqôl gāḏôl) matches the communal scale of the commitment: no one can claim ignorance of what was promised on behalf of all Judah on this day.
15: All Judah rejoices concerning the oath (wayyiśmᵉḥû kol-Yᵉhûḏāh ʿal-hašŠᵉḇûʿāh), for they have sworn with all their heart and have sought him with all their desire (ûḇᵉḵol-ratṣônām biqqᵉšûhû), and he has been found by them. The joy that accompanies the covenant oath is the Chronicler’s affirmation that covenant-keeping is not a burden but a delight. YHWH gives them rest on every side — wayyānaḥ lāhem missāḇîḇ — the direct reward of the seeking-and-finding covenant.
16: King Asa even removes Maacah his grandmother from being queen mother (gᵉḇîrāh) because she had made a horrifying thing (mipleset) for Asherah. The gᵉḇîrāh (queen mother) was an influential political office in the Judahite monarchy — the king’s mother held a court position and wielded real power (1 Kings 2:19; 2 Chronicles 15:13). Asa’s removal of his grandmother from this office for cultic apostasy demonstrates that the reform extends to the royal family itself; no political relationship immunizes against covenant accountability.
17: Asa cuts down her Asherah image (ʾet-miplastāh), beats it small, and burns it at the brook Kidron. The destruction formula — cut down, crush, burn — is the Chronicler’s standard demolition liturgy for cult objects (cf. Josiah’s more elaborate destruction in 2 Chronicles 34:4-7). The Kidron Valley just east of Jerusalem is the city’s waste disposal site for desecrated sacred objects, the place where the unclean is cast (2 Chronicles 29:16; 2 Chronicles 30:14; 2 Kings 23:4-6).
18: Nevertheless, the high places were not removed from Israel. The qualification is significant: despite the extensive reform, the local high places outside Jerusalem survive into Asa’s reign. The repeated note about surviving high places in the Chronicler’s good-king evaluations (14:3 says Asa removed them; here it says they were not removed) may reflect different phases of his reign or different regions — high places in the countryside vs. those in the cities. The heart was wholly devoted to YHWH (šālēm lēḇāḇ ʾAsāʾ kol-yāmāyw) — but external cult reform was incomplete.
19: There was no more war until the thirty-fifth year of Asa’s reign. The 35-year span of peace is the Chronicler’s record of covenant-faithfulness-producing-rest at its longest: from the covenant renewal (year 15, v10) until year 35, two decades of peace. The extended peace is not merely political fortune but covenant theology — YHWH’s gift to the seeking king and people.
